record_office_terminology.land_records: Clerk of Court (conveyances, mortgages, liens — recorded as notarial acts under Louisiana civil law)
record_office_terminology.probate: Clerk of Court — SUCCESSIONS (Louisiana civil law term; NOT 'probate'). Includes inventories, family meetings (conseil de famille), and partitions
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Clerk of Court (parish-level marriage licenses; Catholic church records often earlier)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1914 (statewide registration). New Orleans has birth/death records from 1790s (French/Spanish era). Orleans Parish Board of Health records from 1803.

burned_county_authority: Louisiana State Archives, Guide to Parish Records; WPA Historical Records Survey — Louisiana; FamilySearch Research Wiki — Louisiana Record Losses; LSU Libraries Special Collections — Louisiana Parish Records Guide
administrative_notes: Louisiana was a French colony (1699-1763), Spanish colony (1763-1800), briefly French again (1800-1803), acquired by the US in the Louisiana Purchase (1803), and became the 18th state on April 30, 1812. Louisiana is the ONLY US state that uses parishes instead of counties as its primary governmental subdivision (64 parishes). Louisiana operates under the Napoleonic Code (civil law system), NOT English common law — this profoundly affects all legal records. Property transfers are 'conveyances' (not deeds), estate proceedings are 'successions' (not probate), and notarial records are a primary legal record type unique to Louisiana. The Clerk of Court handles land records, successions, and marriage licenses at the parish level. Catholic Church records are PRIMARY sources for colonial and antebellum Louisiana — the Archdiocese of New Orleans has records from 1718. The Notarial Archives Research Center in New Orleans has 30+ million pages dating to 1733. Louisiana had one of the largest enslaved populations in the US, with unique records under the Code Noir (1724) including church baptismal registers of enslaved persons. The gens de couleur libres (free people of color) community, especially in New Orleans and along Cane River, created extensive property and legal records.

core_repositories.repository: Louisiana State Archives, Office of the Secretary of State, Baton Rouge
core_repositories.description: Louisiana's primary state repository. Houses colonial records (French and Spanish), territorial records, state government records, parish records on microfilm, vital records, military records, land records, newspapers, photographs, and maps. Essential for all Louisiana genealogical research. Collections include parish clerk of court microfilm, colonial documents, Confederate records, and Freedmen's Bureau materials.

core_repositories.source_name: New Orleans Public Library — Louisiana Division
core_repositories.repository: New Orleans Public Library, Main Library, Louisiana Division, New Orleans
core_repositories.description: Major research collection for New Orleans and Louisiana history. City directories from 1805, city archives, photographs, manuscripts, and newspaper collections. Orleans Parish vital records (births from 1790, deaths from 1804). WPA Louisiana Writers' Project materials. Essential for New Orleans family research.

core_repositories.repository: The Historic New Orleans Collection, French Quarter, New Orleans
core_repositories.description: Museum and research center with extensive Louisiana manuscript collections, maps, photographs, and art. Williams Research Center holds plantation records, business records, family papers, and colonial documents. THNOC's online catalog provides access to finding aids for manuscript collections.

core_repositories.repository: Tulane University, Howard-Tilton Memorial Library, New Orleans
core_repositories.description: Major manuscript repository for Louisiana and Gulf South history. Holds plantation records, family papers, business records, political papers, and maps. The Southeastern Architectural Archive has plantation drawings. Hogan Jazz Archive documents New Orleans musical heritage. Essential for antebellum plantation research.

core_repositories.source_name: Notarial Archives Research Center — New Orleans
core_repositories.repository: Notarial Archives Research Center, New Orleans
core_repositories.description: UNIQUE TO LOUISIANA: Over 30 million pages of notarial acts dating from 1733 to the present. Under Louisiana's civil law system, notaries public create original acts (not copies) for property sales, successions, marriage contracts, slave sales, emancipations, business formations, powers of attorney, and more. This is the most comprehensive notarial archive in the Western Hemisphere and an essential resource for all New Orleans and Louisiana research.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections: parish records (conveyances, successions, marriages), Catholic church registers (including enslaved persons and free people of color registers), vital records, cemetery records, and military records. Extensive microfilm partnership with Louisiana State Archives and Archdiocese of New Orleans.

Comprehensive research guide with parish-specific pages, record availability, burned parish information, and links to digitized microfilm. Includes guides for Acadian research, free people of color, Napoleonic Code legal records, and Louisiana's unique civil law terminology.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Louisiana newspapers from the colonial era forward. Includes Le Moniteur de la Louisiane (French-language), New Orleans Daily Picayune, Times-Picayune, Shreveport Times, and parish weeklies. Legal notices, succession advertisements, slave sales, and community news. French and English language papers.

Collaborative digital library from Louisiana libraries, archives, and museums. Includes digitized newspapers, photographs, maps, manuscripts, and oral histories from across the state. Strong coverage of French-language newspapers and African American newspapers.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Louisiana State Archives — Enslaved Persons Records
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Louisiana had one of the largest enslaved populations in the US. Records include notarial acts (slave sales, emancipations, manumissions), succession inventories listing enslaved persons by name/age/value, Catholic church baptismal registers of enslaved persons, Code Noir enforcement records, and plantation records. Sugar parish records are especially rich. The 1795 Pointe Coupee Slave Conspiracy and 1811 German Coast Uprising are well-documented.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.repository: Amistad Research Center, Tulane University, New Orleans
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Major repository for African American history and civil rights. Holds 20+ million documents including American Missionary Association archives, NAACP papers, civil rights organization records, and African American family papers. Essential for researching the African American experience in Louisiana from slavery through the civil rights movement.

Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands for Louisiana (1865-1872). Labor contracts, marriage registrations, school reports, ration records, and complaint files. NARA microfilm M1027 and M1905. Many indexed on FamilySearch. Louisiana's Freedmen's Bureau operated offices throughout the state with headquarters in New Orleans.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Free People of Color Records — New Orleans and Statewide
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.repository: Notarial Archives Research Center / Archdiocese of New Orleans / Louisiana State Archives
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Louisiana's gens de couleur libres (free people of color) created extensive records unique in the US South. Separate Catholic church registers (baptism, marriage, burial), property records (many owned significant real estate), militia records (Battalion of Free Men of Color), notarial acts (business contracts, emancipations), and court records. The Cane River Creole community in Natchitoches Parish and the New Orleans FPC community are especially well-documented. Placage (common-law relationships between white men and women of color) documented in notarial records.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: New Orleans Port of Entry — Passenger Arrival Records
immigration_and_naturalization.description: New Orleans was the 2nd largest US immigration port after New York. Passenger arrival lists from 1820-1945. Major immigrant groups: French, Spanish, German (German Coast settlers, 1720s+), Acadian/Cajun (1755-1785), Irish (1830s-1860s, especially during Famine), Italian (1880s-1920s), Vietnamese (1975+), and Cuban. NARA microfilm M259. Indexed on Ancestry and FamilySearch.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Louisiana Naturalization Records — NARA / Parish Courts
immigration_and_naturalization.repository: NARA Fort Worth / Louisiana State Archives / Parish Clerks of Court
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Naturalization records from Louisiana courts. Parish-level naturalization records at clerks of court (especially Orleans, Jefferson, Caddo, Calcasieu). Federal naturalization records at NARA Fort Worth. Many early naturalizations in French and Spanish. Acadian naturalization records (British subjects becoming American citizens). Isleno (Canary Islander) records.

native_american_and_removal_records.repository: NARA / Louisiana State Archives / Tribal Cultural Centers
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Louisiana has four federally recognized tribes: Chitimacha Tribe (Charenton, St. Mary Parish), Coushatta Tribe (Elton, Allen Parish), Tunica-Biloxi Tribe (Marksville, Avoyelles Parish), and Jena Band of Choctaw (Jena, LaSalle Parish). The United Houma Nation is state-recognized. BIA records at NARA Record Group 75. French and Spanish colonial records document extensive interactions with Natchez, Choctaw, Chitimacha, Atakapa, Caddo, and other peoples. Some Choctaw removal records (1830s) pass through Louisiana.

military_records.description: Confederate pension applications, Confederate service records, War of 1812 records (Battle of New Orleans, 1815), Louisiana militia records, Spanish-American War records, and WWI/WWII records. Louisiana Confederate soldiers served in units like the Washington Artillery, Louisiana Tigers, and Pelican Rifles. Confederate pension files include family details and residence history.

Federal military records: War of 1812 (Battle of New Orleans), Mexican-American War, Civil War (Confederate and Union — including Native Guards, Black troops), Indian Wars, and later conflicts. Fort Polk, Fort Livingston, and Jackson Barracks records. Compiled Military Service Records for Louisiana Confederate soldiers. US Colored Troops records for Louisiana regiments.

land_and_property.source_name: French and Spanish Colonial Land Grants — Louisiana State Land Office
land_and_property.repository: Louisiana State Archives / State Land Office / BLM General Land Office
land_and_property.description: French colonial concessions (1699-1763), Spanish grants (1763-1800), and US confirmations of colonial grants. Bureau of Land Management General Land Office Records include Louisiana homestead patents, cash entries, and military bounty land warrants. Louisiana State Land Office records. Louisiana's civil law system means property records follow different conventions than common-law states.

land_and_property.repository: Parish Clerks of Court / Notarial Archives Research Center (New Orleans)
land_and_property.description: Under Louisiana civil law, property transfers are executed by notaries and filed with the parish clerk of court. These notarial acts are more detailed than common law deeds — they include the full legal description, price, terms, and often family relationships. Notarial records also include marriage contracts (setting property terms), successions, and business records. Each parish maintains its own notarial index. The New Orleans Notarial Archives are by far the largest collection.

vital_records.source_name: Louisiana Department of Health — Vital Records Registry
vital_records.repository: Louisiana Department of Health, Vital Records Registry, New Orleans
vital_records.description: State vital records: statewide birth and death registration from 1914. New Orleans has birth records from 1790 and death records from 1804 (French/Spanish colonial era). Pre-1914 vital events outside New Orleans must be found in Catholic/Protestant church records, parish clerk records, newspaper notices, or cemetery records. Orleans Parish Board of Health records from 1803 at Louisiana State Archives.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives — Catholic Church Records
church_and_cemetery.repository: Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives, New Orleans
church_and_cemetery.description: CRITICAL for Louisiana research: Catholic church sacramental records from 1718. St. Louis Cathedral records are among the oldest church records in the US. Baptism, marriage, and burial registers — with separate registers for enslaved persons and free people of color. Records in French, Spanish, and Latin. The archdiocese archives holds records for parishes across south Louisiana. Some records also available through FamilySearch microfilm.

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Louisiana. Coverage varies by parish — older Acadian Catholic cemeteries and New Orleans above-ground cemeteries have extensive transcriptions. St. Louis Cemeteries No. 1, 2, and 3 (New Orleans) are among the most documented. Save Our Cemeteries organization (New Orleans) has additional documentation.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society. Publishes the Louisiana Genealogical Register. Maintains research resources, sponsors conferences, and coordinates with parish genealogical societies across the state.
Volunteer-contributed parish-level genealogy resources including cemetery transcriptions, Bible records, parish histories, and research guides for all 64 Louisiana parishes.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Louisiana has several extinct or reorganized jurisdictions. The original Orleans Territory (1804-1812) was divided into 12 counties in 1805 (Acadia, Attakapas, Concordia, German Coast, Iberville, Lafourche, Natchitoches, Opelousas, Orleans, Ouachita, Pointe Coupee, and Rapides), which were reorganized into 19 parishes in 1807. The Florida Parishes (from Spanish West Florida) were added in 1810. Carroll Parish (created 1832) was divided into East Carroll and West Carroll in 1877. Feliciana Parish (created 1810) was divided into East Feliciana and West Feliciana in 1824. The original Attakapas County became St. Martin and St. Mary parishes. German Coast County became St. Charles and St. John the Baptist. Opelousas County became St. Landry. These name changes are critical when tracing early Louisiana records.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Louisiana's record losses stem from courthouse fires, Civil War destruction, and hurricanes. The most significant losses: Orleans Parish (1788 and 1794 colonial fires; Hurricane Katrina 2005); Rapides Parish (Union Army burned Alexandria, 1864); Catahoula Parish (1851 and 1882 fires); St. Bernard Parish (Hurricane Katrina 2005 — devastating); Plaquemines Parish (Hurricane Katrina 2005); Calcasieu Parish (1910 fire); Cameron Parish (Hurricane Audrey 1957, Hurricane Rita 2005); Grant Parish (1878 fire); Concordia Parish (Civil War and flooding); and Bienville Parish (courthouse fire). For burned parishes, the Louisiana State Archives maintains microfilm copies of many parish records. Catholic church records (Archdiocese of New Orleans, Diocese of Lafayette, Diocese of Baton Rouge) often provide alternative documentation predating civil records.
